title: Introduction to MazamaRollUtils
text: Time series analysis often uses rolling calculations moving averages. functions straightforward write language makes sense C++ versions common rolling functions available R dramatically speed calculations. Several packages exist provide version functionality: zoo – widely used package ordered observations rolling calculations seismicRoll – rolling functions focused seismology RcppRoll – rolling functions basic statistics goal creating new package C++ rolling functions build suite functions useful environmental time series analysis. want functions available neutral environment underlying data model. functions straightforward use reasonably possible target audience data analysts level R expertise.

title: Introduction to MazamaRollUtils
text: Install CRAN : Install latest version GitHub :
code: install.packages('MazamaRollUtils') remotes::install_github("MazamaScience/MazamaRollUtils")

title: Introduction to MazamaRollUtils
text: Many rolling functions MazamaRollUtils names familiar R functions roll_ prepended. functions calculate rolling versions expected statistic: roll_max() roll_mean() roll_median() roll_min() roll_prod() roll_sd() roll_sum() roll_var() Additional rolling functions equivalent base R include: roll_MAD() – Median Absolute Deviation roll_hampel() – Hampel filter roll_nowcast() – US EPA NowCast functions wrap rolling functions provide enhanced functionality. required return vectors length input data. findOutliers() – returns indices outlier values identified roll_hampel().

title: Introduction to MazamaRollUtils
text: roll_~() functions accept arguments appropriate: x – Numeric vector input. width – Integer width rolling window. – Integer shift use sliding window next location align – Character position return value within window. One : "left" | "center" | "right". na.rm – Logical specifying whether NA values removed calculations within window. roll_mean() function also accepts: weights – Numeric vector size width specifying window index weight. NULL, unit weights used. statistical clarity, roll_sd() roll_var() provide na.rm argument. roll_nowcast() function accepts x argument defined : width = 12 = 1 align = "right" na.rm = TRUE

title: Introduction to MazamaRollUtils
text: output roll_~() function guaranteed length input vector, varying stretches NA one ends depending arguments width, align na.rm. makes easy align return values input data.

title: Introduction to MazamaRollUtils
text: example dataset included package contains tiny amount data suffices demonstrate usage package functions.

title: Introduction to MazamaRollUtils
code: library(MazamaRollUtils)  # Extract vectors from our example dataset t <- example_pm25$datetime x <- example_pm25$pm25  # Plot with 3- and 24-hr rolling means layout(matrix(seq(2))) plot(t, x, pch = 16, cex = 0.5) lines(t, roll_mean(x, width = 3), col = 'red') title("3-hour Rolling Mean") plot(t, x, pch = 16, cex = 0.5) lines(t, roll_mean(x, width = 24), col = 'red') title("24-hour Rolling Mean") layout(1)

title: Introduction to MazamaRollUtils
text: next example uses standard arguments quickly calculate daily maximum value spread across indices.
code: library(MazamaRollUtils)  # Extract vectors from our example dataset t <- example_pm25$datetime x <- example_pm25$pm25  # Calculate the left-aligned 24-hr max every hour, ignoring NA values max_24hr <- roll_max(x, width = 24, align = "left", by = 1, na.rm = TRUE)  # Calculate the left-aligned daily max once every 24 hours, ignoring NA values max_daily_day <- roll_max(x, width = 24, align = "left", by = 24, na.rm = TRUE)  # Spread the max_daily_day value out to every hour with a right-aligned look "back" max_daily_hour <- roll_max(max_daily_day, width = 24, align = "right", by = 1, na.rm = TRUE)  # Plot with 3- and 24-hr rolling means layout(matrix(seq(3))) plot(t, max_24hr, col = 'red') points(t, x, pch = 16, cex = 0.5) title("Rolling 24-hr Max") plot(t, max_daily_day, col = 'red') points(t, x, pch = 16, cex = 0.5) title("Daily 24-hr Max") plot(t, max_daily_hour, col = 'red') points(t, x, pch = 16, cex = 0.5) title("Hourly Daily Max") layout(1)

title: Introduction to MazamaRollUtils
text: roll_mean() function accepts weights argument can used create weighted moving average. next example demonstrates creation exponential weighting function applied data.
code: library(MazamaRollUtils)  # Extract vectors from our example dataset t <- example_pm25$datetime x <- example_pm25$pm25  # Create weights for a 9-element exponentially weighted window #   See:  https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Moving_average N <- 9 alpha <- 2/(N + 1) w <- (1-alpha)^(0:(N-1)) weights <- rev(w)          # right aligned window  EMA <- roll_mean(x, width = N, align = "right", weights = weights)  # Plot Exponential Moving Average (EMA) plot(t, x, pch = 16, cex = 0.5) lines(t, EMA, col = 'red') title("9-Element Exponential Moving Average")

title: Efficient Rolling Functions
code: A suite of compiled functions calculating rolling mins, means, maxes and other statistics. This package is designed to meet the needs of data processing systems for environmental time series.

title: Efficient Rolling Functions
text: Analysis time series data often involves applying “rolling” functions calculate, e.g. “moving average”. functions straightforward write language makes sense C++ versions common rolling functions available R dramatically speed calculations. Several packages exist provide version functionality: zoo – core R package specific data model seismicRoll – rolling functions focused seismology RcppRoll – rolling functions basic statistics goal creating new package C++ rolling functions build suite functions useful environmental time series analysis. want functions available neutral environment underlying data model. functions straightforward use reasonably possible target audience data analysts level R expertise.

title: Efficient Rolling Functions
text: Install CRAN : Install latest version GitHub :
code: install.packages('MazamaRollUtils') devtools::install_github("MazamaScience/MazamaRollUtils")

title: Efficient Rolling Functions
text: project supported USFS AirFire team.
code: library(MazamaRollUtils)  # Example air quality time series t <- example_pm25$datetime x <- example_pm25$pm25  plot(t, x) lines(t, roll_max(x, width = 12), col = 'salmon') lines(t, roll_min(x, width = 12), col = 'light blue')

title: Example timeseries dataset — example_pm25
text: example_pm25 dataset provides example timeseries data practicing code examples. dataset represents hourly air quality measurements. dataset generated 2021-09-22 running:
code: library(AirSensor)  example_pm25 <- example_sensor$data names(example_pm25) <- c("datetime", "pm25")  save(example_pm25, file = "data/example_pm25.rda")

title: Example timeseries dataset — example_pm25
code: example_pm25

title: Example timeseries dataset — example_pm25
text: data frame 2 columns: datetime POSIXct timestamp observation pm25 PM2.5 concentration (µg/m³)

what: Outlier detection with a rolling Hampel filter — findOutliers
title: Outlier detection with a rolling Hampel filter — findOutliers
text: wrapper around roll_hampel() identifies outliers using either fixed threshold threshold derived input data.

title: Outlier detection with a rolling Hampel filter — findOutliers
code: findOutliers(   x,   width = 25,   thresholdMin = 7,   selectivity = NA,   fixedThreshold = TRUE )

title: Outlier detection with a rolling Hampel filter — findOutliers
text: x Numeric vector. width Integer width rolling window. thresholdMin Numeric threshold outlier detection selectivity Value 0 1 used determining outliers, NA fixedThreshold=TRUE. fixedThreshold Logical specifying whether outlier detection uses selectivity  (see Details).

title: Outlier detection with a rolling Hampel filter — findOutliers
text: vector indices associated outliers incoming data x.

title: Outlier detection with a rolling Hampel filter — findOutliers
text: thresholdMin level similar sigma value normally distributed data. Hampel filter values 6 indicate data value extremely unlikely part normal distribution  (~ 1/500 million) therefore likely outlier. choosing relatively large value thresholdMin make less likely generate false positives. False positives can include high frequency environmental noise. default setting fixedThreshold = TRUE value threshold considered outlier selectivity ignored. selectivity value 0 1 used generate appropriate threshold outlier detection based Hampel filter values computed incoming data. lower value selectivity result outliers, value closer 1.0 result fewer. fixedThreshold=TRUE, selectivity may value NA. user specifies fixedThreshold=FALSE, thresholdMin selectivity parameters work like squelch volume CB radio: thresholdMin sets noise threshold want anything returned selectivity adjusts number points defined outliers setting new threshold defined maximum value roll_hampel multiplied selectivity. width, window width, parameter passed roll_hampel().

title: Outlier detection with a rolling Hampel filter — findOutliers
text: function copied seismicRoll package.

title: Outlier detection with a rolling Hampel filter — findOutliers
code: # Noisy sinusoid with outliers a <- jitter(sin(0.1*seq(1e4)),amount=0.2) indices <- sample(seq(1e4),20) a[indices] <- a[indices]*10  # Outlier detection should identify many of these altered indices sort(indices) #>  [1]  552  766  795 1613 1761 2167 2245 2794 3086 3565 3636 4689 5329 5638 5885 #> [16] 6483 6877 7809 8182 9411 o_indices <- findOutliers(a) o_indices #>  [1]  552  766  795 1613 2245 3086 3565 3636 4689 5329 5638 5885 6483 7809 8182 #> [16] 9411  plot(a) points(o_indices, a[o_indices], pch = 16, cex = 0.8, col = 'red') title("Outlier detection using a Hampel filter")

title: Roll MAD — roll_MAD
text: Apply moving-window Median Absolute Deviation function numeric vector.

title: Roll MAD — roll_MAD
code: roll_MAD(   x,   width = 1L,   by = 1L,   align = c("center", "left", "right"),   na.rm = FALSE )

title: Roll MAD — roll_MAD
text: x Numeric vector. width Integer width rolling window. Integer shift window moved iteration. align Character position return value within window. One : "left" | "center" | "right". na.rm Logical specifying whether NA values removed calculations within window.

title: Roll MAD — roll_MAD
text: Numeric vector length x.

title: Roll MAD — roll_MAD
text: every index incoming vector x, value returned Median Absolute Deviation (MAD) values x fall within window width width. align parameter determines alignment return value within window. Thus: align = "left"   [*------] cause returned vector width - 1 NA values right end. align = "center" [---*---] cause returned vector NA values either end needed centered alignment. align = "right"  [------*] cause returned vector width - 1 NA values left end. large vectors, parameter can used force window jump ahead indices next calculation. Indices skipped assigned NA values return vector still length incoming vector. can dramatically speed calculations high resolution time series data.

title: Roll MAD — roll_MAD
code: # Wikipedia example x <- c(0, 0, 0, 1, 1, 2, 2, 4, 6, 9, 0, 0, 0) roll_MAD(x, 3) #>  [1] NA  0  0  0  0  0  0  2  2  3  0  0 NA roll_MAD(x, 5) #>  [1] NA NA  0  1  1  1  1  2  2  4  0 NA NA roll_MAD(x, 7) #>  [1] NA NA NA  1  1  1  1  2  2  2 NA NA NA

title: Roll Max — roll_max
code: # Example air quality time series t <- example_pm25$datetime x <- example_pm25$pm25  plot(t, x, pch = 16, cex = 0.5) lines(t, roll_max(x, width = 12), col = "red") lines(t, roll_min(x, width = 12), col = "deepskyblue") title("12-hr Rolling Max and Min")   plot(t, x, pch = 16, cex = 0.5) points(t, roll_max(x, width = 12, na.rm = TRUE),        pch = 16, col = "red") points(t, roll_max(x, width = 12, na.rm = FALSE),        pch = 16, col = adjustcolor("black", 0.4)) legend("topright", pch = c(1, 16),        col = c("red", adjustcolor("black", 0.4)),        legend = c("na.rm = TRUE", "na.rm = FALSE")) title("12-hr Rolling max with/out na.rm")

title: Roll Mean — roll_mean
text: every index incoming vector x, value returned mean values x fall within window width width. align parameter determines alignment return value within window. Thus: align = "left"   [*------] cause returned vector width - 1 NA values right end. align = "center" [---*---] cause returned vector NA values either end needed centered alignment. align = "right"  [------*] cause returned vector width - 1 NA values left end. large vectors, parameter can used force window jump ahead indices next calculation. Indices skipped assigned NA values return vector still length incoming vector. can dramatically speed calculations high resolution time series data. roll_mean() function supports additional weights argument can used calculate weighted moving average, convolution incoming data kernel provided weights.

title: Roll Mean — roll_mean
code: # Example air quality time series t <- example_pm25$datetime x <- example_pm25$pm25  plot(t, x, pch = 16, cex = 0.5) lines(t, roll_mean(x, width = 3), col = "goldenrod") lines(t, roll_mean(x, width = 23), col = "purple") legend("topright", lty = c(1, 1),        col = c("goldenrod", "purple"),        legend = c("3-hr mean", "23-hr mean")) title("3- and 23-hr Rolling mean")

title: Roll NowCast — roll_nowcast
text: Apply EPA NowCast algorithm numeric vector hourly particulate matter measurements.

title: Roll NowCast — roll_nowcast
code: roll_nowcast(x)

title: Roll NowCast — roll_nowcast
text: x Numeric vector hourly PM measurements.

title: Roll NowCast — roll_nowcast
text: Numeric vector length x.

title: Roll NowCast — roll_nowcast
text: EPA NowCast weighted average designed emphasize recent hourly PM values still using previous 12 hours data. weighting depends much concentrations vary within window: rapidly changing conditions place weight recent hours, stable conditions allow older hours contribute evenly. every index incoming vector x, value returned NowCast associated hour. calculation always right-aligned: [-----------*] * marks hour receiving NowCast value. Early values use available data back beginning series, first 11 positions may calculated fewer 12 hours. Missing values allowed, least 2 valid values must present recent 3 hours result index NA. Returned values rounded one decimal place.

title: Roll Product — roll_prod
text: Apply moving-window product function numeric vector.

title: Roll Product — roll_prod
code: roll_prod(   x,   width = 1L,   by = 1L,   align = c("center", "left", "right"),   na.rm = FALSE )

title: Roll Product — roll_prod
text: x Numeric vector. width Integer width rolling window. Integer shift window moved iteration. align Character position return value within window. One : "left" | "center" | "right". na.rm Logical specifying whether NA values removed calculations within window.

title: Roll Product — roll_prod
text: Numeric vector length x.

title: Roll Product — roll_prod
text: every index incoming vector x, value returned product values x fall within window width width. align parameter determines alignment return value within window. Thus: align = "left"   [*------] cause returned vector width - 1 NA values right end. align = "center" [---*---] cause returned vector NA values either end needed centered alignment. align = "right"  [------*] cause returned vector width - 1 NA values left end. large vectors, parameter can used force window jump ahead indices next calculation. Indices skipped assigned NA values return vector still length incoming vector. can dramatically speed calculations high resolution time series data.
